stories.title: A Solo Founder Just Sold His 6-Month-Old AI Startup for $80M
stories.summary: Maor Shlomo built Base44, an AI app-building platform, entirely solo with just $10K-20K of his own money. Within six months, he reached 250,000 users, turned profitable, and sold the company to Wix for $80 million.
stories.extended_summary: Maor Shlomo took $10K-20K from his own pocket and built Base44 completely alone — a platform where anyone could type prompts and watch full applications materialize. Six months later, he had 250,000 users, the company was profitable, and Wix wrote him a check for $80 million. No co-founder, no VC meetings, no pitch decks. Just one person, a vibe-coding vision, and an exit that most startups spend years chasing.

stories.title: How an Indie Hacker Built an AI Headshot Business to $300K/Month
stories.summary: Danny Postma taught himself to code at 25 and built HeadshotPro, an AI headshot generator, in just 30 hours. The tool earned $100K in its first two weeks and now generates $300K monthly, making him a solo founder earning $3.6M annually from Bali.
stories.extended_summary: Danny Postma was a Dutch web marketer who didn't write his first line of code until 25. He spotted Stable Diffusion's potential, locked in for 30 hours, and emerged with HeadshotPro — an AI that generates professional headshots. Two weeks after launch, he'd made $100K. Now he pulls $300K a month, $3.6 million a year, working solo from Bali. No team, no office, no traditional path — just a self-taught coder who saw the moment and moved.

stories.title: He Left Medicine to Build an AI Tool — Now, It's Worth $460 Million
stories.summary: Dr. Thomas Kelly left medicine after crushing burnout — seeing 100 patients a day with only 10 minutes each. He built Heidi, an AI medical scribe that automates clinical documentation, now valued at $465 million and supporting 1.8 million patient interactions weekly across 50 countries.
stories.extended_summary: Dr. Thomas Kelly graduated from medical school in Melbourne in 2017 and immediately hit a wall: 100 patients a day, 10 minutes each, drowning in documentation. He walked away in 2021 to build Heidi, an AI scribe that handles the clinical paperwork doctors hate. Four years later, Heidi is valued at $465 million, raised $65M in Series B, and supports 1.8 million patient interactions every week across 50 countries. He left medicine to fix it.

stories.title: From Viral Side Project to a $5M/yr B2B AI Platform
stories.summary: Yasser had 16 Twitter followers when he tweeted about Chatbase, a tool that lets users chat with their documents. The tweet went viral, and within two years he scaled the side project to $5M annual revenue, even after failing two final classes to pursue it full-time.
stories.extended_summary: Yasser was a final-year university student with 16 Twitter followers when he posted about Chatbase — a simple tool that lets you upload documents and chat with them. The tweet exploded. He failed two final classes because he couldn't stop building, and two years later Chatbase is pulling $5 million in annual recurring revenue. Sixteen followers to a multi-million-dollar business. Sometimes one tweet is all it takes.

stories.title: Hitting $30K MRR with an AI Marketing Product
stories.summary: After wasting three months on a failed consumer product, Richard built Leadmore AI — an AI-powered Reddit marketing tool — in just over a month. He spent $0 on marketing, used Reddit itself as his distribution channel, and hit $30K monthly revenue in four months before crossing $1M annually.
stories.extended_summary: Richard spent three months building a consumer product that went nowhere. He pivoted, built Leadmore AI — a tool that automates Reddit marketing — in just over a month, and decided to market it on Reddit itself. Zero dollars spent on ads. Four months later, he was making $30K a month. Within a year, he crossed $1 million in annual revenue. Sometimes the best marketing channel is the one your product was built for.

The Y Combinator-backed startup hit seven-figure annual revenue in under a year and now serves 50+ customers across six continents.
stories.extended_summary: Madhu Nadig and Baran Ozkan watched banks struggle with outdated anti-money laundering software that couldn't keep up with modern fraud. They co-founded Flagright, the first compliance platform built natively around generative AI, and got into Y Combinator. Less than a year later, they hit seven figures in annual revenue and now serve over 50 customers on six continents. Legacy systems couldn't adapt fast enough — so they built the alternative.

stories.title: Hitting $2M ARR in Two Years Even Though He Was 'Late to the AI Party'
stories.summary: Dominic Zijlstra, a former space engineer at Airbus who taught himself Mandarin and built a language learning SaaS, thought he was too late for the AI wave. He built Adaptify, an AI-powered SEO automation platform for agencies, and reached $2M annual revenue in exactly two years as a bootstrapped founder.
stories.extended_summary: Dominic Zijlstra was a space engineer at Airbus who taught himself Mandarin and built a language learning SaaS on the side. When AI exploded, he thought he'd missed the window. He built Adaptify anyway — an AI-powered SEO automation tool for agencies — and bootstrapped it without outside funding. Two years to the day later, he hit $2 million in annual recurring revenue. Turns out, you're never too late if you actually ship.

stories.title: Hitting Six Figures Within Three Months of Launching an AI Bootcamp
stories.summary: Ben Issen, who was Webflow's first intern at 18, teamed up with Zineb Salamat to co-found Side School, an AI bootcamp teaching practical AI skills through challenge-based learning. The Paris-based team bootstrapped to six-figure revenue within three months and grew to thousands of students.
stories.extended_summary: Ben Issen started as Webflow's first intern when he was just 18. Years later, he and Zineb Salamat launched Side School, an AI bootcamp that ditches lectures for hands-on challenges. They bootstrapped the whole thing from Paris with a team of five. Three months in, they hit six figures in revenue. Now they've got thousands of students learning AI by actually building things. No fluff, just reps.

stories.title: Entrepreneur of 2025: Adeel Khan, Founder and CEO, MagicSchool AI, Denver
stories.summary: Adeel Khan, who founded Denver's top-performing public high school, built MagicSchool AI after watching teachers struggle to learn ChatGPT. Starting with a $25,000 angel check, his platform now serves 5.5 million educators in 13,000+ schools and raised $65M — the fastest-growing technology product for schools ever.
stories.extended_summary: Adeel Khan founded Denver's top-performing public high school as a former Teach for America corps member, so he knew exactly how overwhelmed teachers were when ChatGPT dropped. He built MagicSchool AI to make AI actually usable in classrooms. He started with a $25,000 angel check. Now the platform serves 5.5 million educators across 13,000+ schools, raised $65 million in venture capital, and is officially the fastest-growing edtech product in history. Teachers needed help — he built it.

stories.title: The 18-Year-Old Dropout Who Raised $6.2M to Build the AI Teammate
stories.summary: Arlan Rakhmetzhanov, an 18-year-old from Kazakhstan, dropped out of high school to move to London, cold-emailed his way into Stanford research, and applied to Y Combinator three times before being accepted. His startup Nozomio, an AI context engine for engineers, raised $6.2M backed by Paul Graham and CRV.
stories.extended_summary: Arlan Rakhmetzhanov was 18, from Almaty, Kazakhstan, and decided high school wasn't worth finishing. He moved to London, cold-emailed his way into a Stanford research gig, and applied to Y Combinator three times — rejected twice. The third time, they said yes. His startup Nozomio builds an AI context engine that helps engineers work faster. He raised $6.2 million in seed funding with backing from Paul Graham and CRV. Eighteen years old, no diploma, and investors writing checks.

stories.title: Cornell Tech Studio Startup Hyro Raises $45 Million to Scale Voice AI in Healthcare
stories.summary: Two Cornell Tech students met in an AI course and turned a class project into Hyro, a voice AI company helping patients navigate healthcare. Seven years later, they serve 45 major health systems and have raised $95 million.
stories.extended_summary: Israel Krush and Rom Cohen bonded over voice AI in a Cornell Tech course and decided to build something real: conversational agents that could help patients book appointments, find doctors, and navigate complex healthcare systems. What started as a Startup Studio project in 2018 became Hyro, now serving 45 large healthcare organizations including Baptist Health and Intermountain Health. The company has raised $95 million total, proving that a classroom idea can scale into infrastructure that makes care more accessible for millions.

stories.title: Automating the Infrastructure of Care: A Conversation with Athena Doshi, CEO of ExactRx
stories.summary: Athena Doshi, a neuroscience grad who started her first company in college, built ExactRx after discovering surgical centers were losing hundreds of thousands to manual tasks. Her AI checklists cut prep time by 30 minutes per case and recover over $300k annually per facility.
stories.extended_summary: Athena Doshi walked into outpatient surgical centers and saw staff drowning in paperwork, losing half an hour per surgery to manual checklists—and hemorrhaging over $300,000 in revenue yearly. She built ExactRx, AI-powered autonomous checklists that reclaim that time and money, giving clinicians 30 minutes back per case to focus on patients instead of admin. A neuroscience graduate from UC San Diego who launched her first startup in college, Doshi was named to Inc.'s 2025 Female Founders 500 for turning operational chaos into streamlined care.

stories.summary: Mutembei Kariuki built Fastagger in Kenya after investors doubted African AI talent. His business intelligence assistant Auni integrates with M-PESA to give small business owners actionable insights—over 600 MSMEs signed up within a month of launch.
stories.extended_summary: Mutembei Kariuki pitched Kenyan investors on an AI assistant for small businesses and heard the same refrain: Africa doesn't have the talent for this. He built it anyway. Fastagger's Auni integrates with M-PESA, Kenya's mobile money platform, to give micro and small business owners real-time insights into their revenue, expenses, and growth opportunities. After partnering with Safaricom, over 600 MSMEs adopted Auni within a month, proving that the technology—and the talent—were there all along.

stories.title: Alexander Tsado: The Architect of Africa's AI Future
stories.summary: Alexander Tsado, a former NVIDIA product manager from Nigeria, launched Africa's first indigenous GPU platform offering compute up to 40% cheaper than global providers. His vision: a GPU in every African village so farmers, students, and health workers can access AI tools.
stories.extended_summary: Alexander Tsado left NVIDIA in February 2025 with a bold goal: put affordable AI compute into the hands of every African community. By June, he had activated the Africa GPU Hub, the continent's first indigenous platform offering GPU access up to 40% cheaper than global providers. A product manager from Benin City, Nigeria, Tsado envisions farmers using AI to predict crop yields, students training models in rural schools, and health workers diagnosing diseases with machine learning—all powered by local infrastructure that puts Africa's AI future in African hands.

stories.title: Who Is Carina Hong, the 24-Year-Old Stanford Dropout Drawing Big Tech Talent to Her AI Math Startup?
stories.summary: Carina Hong, a 24-year-old mathematician, dropped out of Stanford's PhD program to found Axiom Math, an AI startup advancing mathematical reasoning. She raised an extraordinary $64 million in seed funding and is pulling top researchers from major tech companies.
stories.extended_summary: Carina Hong was deep into a Stanford PhD in mathematics when she decided to leave. At 24, the China-born mathematician founded Axiom Math to push AI's ability to solve complex mathematical problems—the kind that stump even advanced researchers. She raised an extraordinary $64 million in seed funding, one of the largest rounds for a math-focused AI startup, and began attracting top researchers from major tech companies who believed in her vision. Her bet: that teaching AI to reason mathematically will unlock breakthroughs far beyond numbers.

stories.title: ClimateAI Is Building the Industry's First Enterprise Climate Planning Platform
stories.summary: Himanshu Gupta grew up in Vrindavan, India, where droughts devastated his community. After drafting India's Renewable Energy chapter and working with Al Gore, he co-founded ClimateAI at Stanford, using machine learning for enterprise climate risk planning.
stories.extended_summary: Himanshu Gupta watched droughts and heatwaves destroy farms in his hometown of Vrindavan, India, and spent his career trying to stop it. He drafted India's Renewable Energy chapter in its National Five Year Plan, worked with Al Gore on emissions modeling, then co-founded ClimateAI at Stanford with Max Evans. The company uses machine learning to help enterprises plan for climate risk—predicting disruptions to supply chains, agriculture, and infrastructure before they happen. ClimateAI was named a TIME Best Invention for turning climate science into actionable strategy.

stories.title: This Kashmiri Entrepreneur Is Transforming Global Agriculture with AI
stories.summary: Dr. Tavseef Mairaj Shah, a Kashmiri environmental engineer trained in Germany, left a steel career to co-found CinSOIL, building AI tools to monitor soil carbon and accelerate regenerative agriculture. He works at the nexus of science, policy, and technology to transform global farming.
stories.extended_summary: Dr. Tavseef Mairaj Shah was working in steel when he realized the real transformation needed to happen underground—in soil. Trained as an environmental engineer in Germany with over a decade in research including stints at the UN FAO and WWF, Shah co-founded CinSOIL to build AI tools that monitor soil carbon stocks and help farmers shift to regenerative practices. His work sits at the intersection of science, policy, and technology, turning soil health from an abstract concept into measurable data that can reshape agriculture globally.

stories.title: Pachama: How an Argentine Founder Used AI and Satellites to Fight Deforestation
stories.summary: Diego Saez-Gil witnessed deforestation in the Amazon and founded Pachama in 2018 to use AI and satellites to verify forest carbon credits. After graduating from Y Combinator, the company was acquired by Carbon Direct in November 2025.
stories.extended_summary: Diego Saez-Gil, an Argentine tech entrepreneur, saw the Amazon rainforest disappearing and knew the carbon credit market needed better verification. In 2018, he founded Pachama to use AI and satellite imagery to measure and verify how much carbon forests actually capture—bringing transparency to an industry rife with greenwashing. After graduating from Y Combinator, Pachama pioneered digital verification for forest carbon credits, proving that technology could hold conservation projects accountable. In November 2025, Carbon Direct acquired the company, integrating its tools into a broader climate strategy.

stories.title: AI for Large-Scale Social Impact: Rahul Panicker's Journey from Infant Warmers to AI for the Developing World
stories.summary: Rahul Panicker co-founded Embrace to create a low-cost infant warmer costing less than 1% of a traditional incubator, helping over 500,000 vulnerable babies in 25+ countries. He now leads AI solutions for underserved populations at Wadhwani AI.
stories.extended_summary: Rahul Panicker, a Stanford PhD graduate, co-founded Embrace after learning that premature and low-birthweight babies in the developing world were dying because traditional incubators cost tens of thousands of dollars. His team built an infant warmer costing less than 1% of that—a simple, effective device that has helped over 500,000 vulnerable babies across 25+ countries survive their first critical days. Now Chief Innovation Officer at Wadhwani AI, Panicker directs AI solutions for healthcare, agriculture, and education in underserved populations, scaling impact from one invention to entire systems.

stories.summary: Danny Postma spent four years earning $0 from Landingfolio before pivoting to AI. Eight months later, he sold his copywriting tool Headlime for $1 million — proving that persistence through zero-revenue years can lead to breakthrough product-market fit.
stories.extended_summary: For four years, Danny Postma's Landingfolio earned exactly zero dollars. Most founders would have quit. Instead, he kept building, kept learning, and eventually pivoted to AI copywriting. Eight months after launching Headlime, he sold it for $1 million. His journey captures the unglamorous reality of indie hacking: years of silence before the breakthrough, the discipline to keep shipping when nothing works, and the instinct to recognize when you've finally found it.

stories.title: MagicSchool's Founder on the Potential and Perils of AI in Education
stories.summary: Adeel Khan founded Denver's top public high school, then built MagicSchool AI — now used in nearly every U.S. school district. His platform offers 80+ AI tools for teachers, making him one of education's most influential voices on AI's promise and risks in classrooms.
stories.extended_summary: Before becoming one of education's most powerful AI founders, Adeel Khan built Denver's highest-performing public high school from scratch. Now his creation, MagicSchool AI, has spread to nearly every school district in America. It offers teachers 80+ AI tools for lesson planning and assessment — automating hours of work while raising urgent questions about what happens when algorithms shape how kids learn. Khan sits at the center of both the promise and the anxiety, building the future while wrestling with its consequences.

stories.title: Killing His AI Product After Growing It to $250K ARR Within a Year
stories.summary: Ulric Musset grew his AI code generator Marblism to $250K in annual revenue within a year. Then he killed it. Rather than coast on early traction, he chose to rebuild from scratch — betting that surface-level success was masking deeper product problems.
stories.extended_summary: Most founders dream of hitting $250K in revenue their first year. Ulric Musset did exactly that with Marblism, an AI tool that generates code. Then he shut it down. While others were celebrating his growth, Musset saw something wrong beneath the numbers — early traction that felt hollow, customers who weren't getting what they really needed. So he made the brutal call: kill it and start over. It's the kind of conviction that separates builders chasing metrics from those chasing real product-market fit.

stories.title: Brazilian Drummer Turned AI Music Pioneer: How Geraldo Ramos Built Moises Into a Global Platform
stories.summary: Brazilian drummer Geraldo Ramos built an AI tool to isolate instruments while recording himself. It went viral on ProductHunt, became Moises Music AI, raised $40M, and won Apple's iPad App of the Year — all because he wanted to practice drums better.
stories.extended_summary: Geraldo Ramos just wanted to practice drums. Recording himself in Brazil, he got frustrated trying to separate the drum track from the rest of the song. So he built an AI tool to do it. The tool went viral on ProductHunt. Suddenly, musicians everywhere were using it. That side project became Moises Music AI — which has now raised $40 million and won Apple's iPad App of the Year. Sometimes the biggest companies start with someone trying to solve their own annoying problem on a Tuesday night.

stories.title: Two Howard University Grads Built an AI App to Help Americans Clear Criminal Records
stories.summary: Lawrence Blackmon paid $2,500 to expunge a dismissed college arrest. He and Roger Roman, both Howard grads, built LegalEase — an AI that guides Americans through expungement eligibility and filing in five minutes, removing barriers to clearing criminal records.
stories.extended_summary: Lawrence Blackmon's college arrest was dismissed, but the record stayed on file. Getting it expunged cost him $2,500 — money most people don't have for a crime that never resulted in conviction. He and fellow Howard grad Roger Roman built LegalEase to change that. Their AI walks users through expungement eligibility and petition filing in five minutes, turning a process that requires lawyers and hundreds of dollars into something anyone can do from their phone. It's tech solving a problem the legal system created and never bothered to fix.
stories.headline_split: Two Howard University Grads Built an AI App to Help Americans
stories.fake_headline: Stanford Dropouts Launch AI Platform That Matches Foster Youth With Mentors, Reaches 10,000 Kids

stories.title: From Admin Assistant to AI Unicorn: How Minna Song Built EliseAI Into a $2.2 Billion Proptech Company
stories.summary: Minna Song was an admin assistant at a real estate firm when she had the idea for EliseAI. She and co-founder Tony Stoyanov built AI to automate leasing conversations — now serving 70% of the top 50 U.S. rental operators at a $2.2 billion valuation.
stories.extended_summary: Minna Song wasn't a tech founder or a VC-backed entrepreneur. She was an administrative assistant at a real estate company, watching property managers drown in repetitive leasing questions. She saw the problem from the inside: hours wasted answering the same texts about rent and pet policies. So she and co-founder Tony Stoyanov built EliseAI to automate those conversations. Today, their company serves 70% of the 50 largest rental housing operators in the U.S. and is valued at $2.2 billion. It's a reminder that the best startup ideas often come from the people doing the work, not the people managing it.

stories.title: A Fourth-Generation Shrimp Trader Built an AI Agent to Fix the Food Supply Chain
stories.summary: Joseph Jacob's great-grandfather was the first to export shrimp from India to the U.S. After buying 400 million pounds of seafood tracked on Excel sheets, Jacob founded Burnt — AI agents that process food orders in six seconds instead of ten minutes.
stories.extended_summary: Joseph Jacob is a fourth-generation shrimp trader. His great-grandfather pioneered the India-to-U.S. seafood trade, and Jacob grew up in the business. After personally buying over 400 million pounds of seafood tracked on Excel spreadsheets, he'd had enough. He founded Burnt to build AI agents that process food orders in six seconds — down from ten minutes of manual work. It's the kind of innovation that only comes from someone who's lived inside a broken system long enough to know exactly where it breaks.

stories.title: Casper Co-Founder Pivots from Sleep to Mental Health, Launches AI Therapist
stories.summary: Neil Parikh co-founded Casper and scaled it past $500M in revenue. Now he's launched Slingshot AI with $93M to build an AI therapist trained on the world's largest behavioral health dataset. In a 10-week trial, 76% of users saw decreased depression symptoms.
stories.extended_summary: Neil Parikh helped build Casper into a $500M+ mattress empire, but sleep wasn't the endgame. Now he's gone after something bigger: mental health. His new company, Slingshot AI, just raised $93 million to build an AI therapist trained on the world's largest behavioral health dataset. In a 10-week trial, 76% of users reported their depression symptoms decreased. It's a bold pivot from selling mattresses to treating the mind — and early results suggest he might actually pull it off.

stories.title: South Africa's First Pediatric Radiologist Built an AI That Reads X-Rays in 25 Milliseconds
stories.summary: Dr. Jaishree Naidoo became South Africa's first pediatric radiologist in 2010. She founded Envisionit Deep AI — the first African-founded company to get FDA clearance for an AI medical device that detects 25 pathologies from chest X-rays in 25 milliseconds.
stories.extended_summary: Dr. Jaishree Naidoo made history twice. First, in 2010, she became South Africa's first qualified pediatric radiologist. Then she built Envisionit Deep AI — an AI that reads chest X-rays and detects 25 different pathologies in 25 milliseconds. Her company became the first African-founded startup to receive FDA clearance for an AI medical device. It's technology that could save lives in underserved areas where radiologists are scarce, built by someone who spent years seeing what happens when diagnosis comes too late.

stories.title: Gen Z Thiel Fellow Raises Backing to Bring AI to America's Industrial Supply Chain
stories.summary: Kiara Nirghin — a Stanford AI researcher, Thiel Fellow, and Google Grand Prize winner — founded Emanate to deploy autonomous revenue agents for the $5 trillion industrial materials sector. She's bringing AI to one of America's oldest, least-digitized supply chains.
stories.extended_summary: Kiara Nirghin won a Google Grand Prize, became a Thiel Fellow, and did AI research at Stanford — all before most people finish their first startup. Now she's founded Emanate to tackle something decidedly unglamorous: the $5 trillion industrial materials supply chain. Her company deploys autonomous revenue agents to automate an industry still running on phone calls and PDFs. It's Gen Z tackling one of America's oldest, least-digitized sectors, armed with AI and the audacity to think she can fix it.

stories.title: Former Disney Executive Launches AI Music Platform for the Taipei Metro
stories.summary: After nearly 20 years overseeing Disney networks across Tokyo, Mumbai, and LA, Anand Roy launched Wubble AI in Singapore. His royalty-free music platform now powers everything from Microsoft and HP campaigns to the entire Taipei Metro system.
stories.extended_summary: The Taipei Metro now soundtracks commuter rides with AI-generated music — no royalties, no licensing headaches. Anand Roy spent two decades running Disney networks in three countries before co-founding Wubble AI in Singapore to solve the music licensing nightmare every brand faces. Microsoft, HP, and L'Oreal have already signed on, but a city's entire transit system choosing AI composition over human artists marks a turning point in how public spaces will sound.

stories.title: Three Spanish Engineers Pivoted to Build AI That Runs Freight Operations
stories.summary: Brothers Pablo and Javier Palafox and friend Luis Paarup moved from Spain to the Bay Area for Y Combinator, then pivoted from computer vision to freight logistics AI. HappyRobot now pulls in over $10M in revenue and just raised a $44M Series B automating the chaos of freight communications.
stories.extended_summary: Three Spanish engineers built computer vision tech, got into Y Combinator, then scrapped it all when they saw the freight industry drowning in emails, texts, and phone calls coordinating shipments. Brothers Pablo and Javier Palafox and their friend Luis Paarup built HappyRobot to automate the endless back-and-forth — now they're processing over $10M in revenue and just closed a $44M Series B. Trucking companies that once needed entire teams to manage freight communications now let AI handle it.

stories.title: A First-Year Lawyer and His DeepMind Roommate Built a Legal AI Startup Now Worth $8 Billion
stories.summary: Winston Weinberg was grinding through his first year as a litigation associate when his DeepMind roommate Gabriel Pereyra suggested testing GPT-3 on landlord-tenant law. Attorneys said 86 of 100 AI answers needed zero edits. Harvey is now an $8 billion legal AI company.
stories.extended_summary: A first-year lawyer and a DeepMind researcher were roommates when they fed GPT-3 some landlord-tenant legal questions just to see what would happen. Attorneys reviewed 100 AI-generated answers and said 86 needed zero edits — not even a comma. Winston Weinberg and Gabriel Pereyra built Harvey around that result, and law firms started using it to draft contracts, research case law, and answer client questions. Three years later, Harvey is valued at $8 billion.

stories.title: Blind Since Birth, This Indian Founder Builds AI for Accessibility
stories.summary: Kartik Sawhney, blind since birth, had to appeal to India's education board just to study science. After Stanford, he managed Microsoft's $25M AI for Accessibility fund and now runs Y Combinator-backed Samora AI, building tech that makes the world more accessible.
stories.extended_summary: Kartik Sawhney was told he couldn't study science because he was blind. He appealed to India's education board — and won. After Stanford, he didn't just build a career in tech; he managed Microsoft's $25M AI for Accessibility fund, directing resources to projects that help disabled people navigate a world designed without them. Now he's leading Samora AI through Y Combinator, building tools that let blind and low-vision users access information the rest of us take for granted.

stories.title: Two SpaceX and Instacart Veterans Built Robot Restaurant Lines Backed by Chipotle
stories.summary: Stephen Klein and Daniel Fukuba built a robotic food truck that launched three months before COVID hit. They pivoted into Hyphen, an AI-powered restaurant makeline that doubles production speed. Chipotle and Cava have invested $35M.
stories.extended_summary: A robotic food truck launched three months before the pandemic — probably the worst timing in startup history. Stephen Klein (ex-Instacart) and Daniel Fukuba (ex-SpaceX) pivoted fast, building Hyphen: an AI-powered restaurant makeline that fits under counters and doubles how many bowls a kitchen can pump out. Chipotle and Cava saw it and invested $35M, betting that the future of fast-casual isn't just faster workers — it's smarter robots doing the repetitive stuff while humans focus on customers.

stories.title: Russian Biophysicist Builds AI That Simulates Skincare Results on Real Skin
stories.summary: Anastasia Georgievskaya studied biophysics at Moscow State University, then spent years analyzing skincare clinical trials. She founded Haut.AI and built SkinGPT, which uses computer vision to simulate how skincare products will actually change your face before you buy them.
stories.extended_summary: Skincare promises are basically fiction — "reduces wrinkles," "evens tone" — with no way to know if it'll work on your actual skin. Anastasia Georgievskaya studied biophysics at Moscow State University and spent years digging through clinical trial data before founding Haut.AI. She built SkinGPT, which analyzes your face with computer vision and simulates what a product will actually do to your skin before you spend $80 on a serum. No more guessing, no more bathroom cabinets full of half-used bottles.

stories.title: Two Ex-Googlers Shut Down Their Profitable Startup When ChatGPT Arrived
stories.summary: Dhruv Amin and Marcus Lowe were running a profitable $2.2M-a-year marketplace when ChatGPT launched. They shut it down, built Anything — a vibe-coding platform — and hit $2M ARR in two weeks with a $100M valuation.
stories.extended_summary: Two ex-Googlers had a $2.2M-a-year profitable business — then ChatGPT dropped and they shut it all down. Dhruv Amin and Marcus Lowe saw the AI shift coming and built Anything, a "vibe-coding" platform that lets people describe what they want and the AI builds it. They hit $2M in annual recurring revenue in two weeks and landed a $100M valuation. Killing a profitable startup to chase something uncertain is terrifying — they did it anyway and it worked.

stories.title: From Cape Town Grocery Delivery to Google-Backed AI Infrastructure
stories.summary: Michael Louis and Jonathan Irwin were working at South African grocery startup OneCart when they realized AI infrastructure was broken. They founded Cerebrium in Cape Town, went through Y Combinator, and raised $8.5M led by Google's Gradient Ventures.
stories.extended_summary: Two engineers at a Cape Town grocery delivery startup kept hitting the same wall: AI infrastructure was too slow, too expensive, and too complicated to actually use. Michael Louis and Jonathan Irwin built Cerebrium to fix it — a platform that makes deploying AI models as simple as pushing code. They got into Y Combinator, moved fast, and raised $8.5M led by Google's Gradient Ventures. A South African startup solving Silicon Valley's AI deployment problem? That's the kind of surprise the tech world needs.

stories.title: A Hollywood Composer Built One of the First AI Music Tools
stories.summary: Drew Silverstein worked on scores for Horrible Bosses and Mass Effect 3, watching producers fight endless licensing battles. He co-founded Amper Music with Sam Estes and Michael Hobe to let anyone create AI-composed music instantly — no royalties, no legal headaches.
stories.extended_summary: Hollywood composer Drew Silverstein worked on Horrible Bosses and Mass Effect 3, and saw the same nightmare every time: producers spending weeks clearing music rights, paying thousands in licensing fees, and still getting stuck with tracks that didn't quite fit. He co-founded Amper Music with Sam Estes and Michael Hobe to build one of the first AI music tools — type in a mood, length, and style, and it generates a custom track in seconds. No royalties, no lawyers, no waiting.

stories.title: A Mexican Soccer Player Turned CEO Brings AI Sales Agents to Latin American E-Commerce
stories.summary: Sergio Garcia was a soccer player in Guadalajara who dropped out of college to build Atrato, scaling it to $20M in revenue before selling. Now he's founded Boom AI, bringing e-commerce AI agents to WhatsApp and TikTok — the only Mexican company in Y Combinator's Fall 2025 batch.
stories.extended_summary: Sergio Garcia was playing soccer in Guadalajara when he dropped out of college to build Atrato, an e-commerce payment platform that hit $20M in revenue before getting acquired. Instead of coasting, he founded Boom AI to bring AI sales agents to Latin American e-commerce — customers shop and checkout entirely through WhatsApp and TikTok. He's the only Mexican founder in Y Combinator's Fall 2025 batch, building tech that meets Latin American shoppers where they already are instead of forcing them into clunky apps.
